text.title: Stakeholder Register Framework
text.summary: Five-step process for identifying, analyzing, mapping, and engaging project stakeholders using the Power-Interest grid.

section.title: Identification
section.p: Identify every person or group who affects or is affected by the project. Include indirect stakeholders often missed:

list.item: Customers and end users (often listed as a group, not individuals)
Regulatory bodies, security council, data privacy, accessibility
"Who can stop this project for 2+ weeks?" — the most important identification question

rule.statement: Identify groups as groups (e.g., "Security Council", "Beta Customers") when a single person is not authoritative.
rule.rationale: Splitting a group into individuals loses the fact that the authority is collective. An agent that lists "Alice, Bob, Carol from Security" implies any one of them can unblock — they cannot.

list.item: Manage Closely (High Power, High Interest) — active engagement, involve in decisions; weekly or more frequent contact
Keep Satisfied (High Power, Low Interest) — regular updates, no surprises; bi-weekly contact
Keep Informed (Low Power, High Interest) — regular communication, gather feedback; weekly status
Monitor (Low Power, Low Interest) — minimal effort, watch for quadrant changes; monthly summary

rule.statement: Use evidence (org chart, budget authority, RACI) for Power rating — not gut feel.
rule.rationale: Two PMs produce different power ratings for the same stakeholder. Anchoring to org-chart position and budget authority makes ratings defensible and consistent across project phases.

rule.statement: Verify attitude through direct conversation, not from written communication inference.
rule.rationale: Written communication (email, Slack) is systematically polite. Agents reading written transcripts will over-classify stakeholders as "Supportive". Only direct conversation reveals resistance.

section.title: Register Maintenance

rule.statement: Refresh the register after each stage gate; archive the previous version to track attitude changes over time.
rule.rationale: A stakeholder who was Low-Low at kickoff can become High-High at launch (e.g., security team). The archived history shows when and why the quadrant shifted — valuable for post-mortems.

list.item: Track last_touch field; automated weekly diff vs comms log flags Manage-Closely stakeholders with zero recent contact
Use the register as input to RACI, Communications Plan, and Risk Register
Privacy: keep PII in a separate access-controlled file; the in-repo register holds only ID and role

section.reference: YAML register schema with all required fields
Individual stakeholder profile with engagement history
